[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.comment: making it so there is only one of this template on every page in [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== Related Topics == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:11]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:Subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:11]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:11]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]][[Category:11]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (泉南市)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the shrines in the region of 泉南市. <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)|ja|泉南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

[[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks]] [[Category:Kujūku Ōji template forks on groups]] [[Category:100 shortest pages as of February 11 2025]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:qqq]][[en:List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:q--q]] [[Category:11]][[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] ]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]][[zh:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Kujūku Ōji}} [[ja:List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)]]

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=99 ōji Shrines}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines--> [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ_Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Sennan City)-qq]] [[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] ==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
