==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->

==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->

==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|12=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=draft}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūkuōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|12=draft}} <!--subst:PAGENAME magic word should be substituted in the future when this is moved to its final pagename. This was added to a few pages not created on Feb 8 because they were very barren and this gives context to the fact that they are in a large group of pages all to be translated more or less as a group similar to the Ise Shrines-->
<!--Sennan City)-->
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|12=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|12=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|12=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|12=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|12=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|12=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|12=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|12=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|12=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|12=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|12=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|12=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.text:
{{Redirect|厩戸王子|厩戸'''皇'''子 |聖徳太子}} 本項目では、[[大阪府]][[泉南市]]内に所在する'''[[九十九王子]]'''（くじゅうくおうじ）について述べる。 == 九十九王子とは == {{main|九十九王子}} 九十九王子（くじゅうくおうじ）とは、[[熊野古道]]、特に[[紀伊路]]沿いに在する[[神社]]のうち、主に[[12世紀]]から[[13世紀]]にかけて、皇族・貴人の熊野詣に際して先達をつとめた熊野修験の手で急速に組織された一群の神社をいい、参詣者の守護が祈願された。 しかしながら、[[承久]]3年（[[1221年]]）の[[承久の乱]]以降、[[京都|京]]からの熊野詣が下火になり、そのルートであった紀伊路が衰退するとともに、荒廃と退転がすすんだ。[[室町時代]]以降、熊野詣がかつてのような卓越した地位を失うにつれ、この傾向はいっそう進み、[[江戸時代|近世]][[紀州藩]]の手による顕彰も行なわれたものの、勢いをとどめるまでには至らなかった。さらに、[[明治]]以降の[[神道]]の[[国家神道]]化とそれに伴う合祀、市街化による廃絶などにより、旧社地が失われたり、比定地が不明になったものも多い。 本記事では、これら九十九王子のうち、比定地が大阪府泉南市内に推定される王子を扱う。 == 九十九王子 == 泉南市内の九十九王子は3社。 === 厩戸王子 === 籾井王子から[[紀州街道]]旧道を西に1.5kmほど進み、海営宮池という池の近くの交差点を北に向かう脇道に入ったところに墓地があり、その北側に石碑と説明板がある。[[大阪府]]指定史跡。 [[建仁]]元年（[[1201年]]）の[[後鳥羽天皇|後鳥羽院]]参詣記（『明月記』所収）10月7日条に「先参厩戸王子」との記述があり、籾井王子にて芸能を奉納したのちに参詣し、信達宿厩戸御所にて宿泊している平凡社[1998: 1527]。。また、[[承元]]4年（[[1210年]]）の[[藤原重子|修明門院]]参詣記（『[[民経記]]』所収）4 月23日条にも、当王子に参詣の後、宿所入りしたとある。 その後、この王子に言及する史料が途絶えるが、[[元文]]元年（[[1736年]]）刊行の『和泉志』に {{quotation|…在中小路村。王子記作馬戸。或曰馬留。今称筆王子。|『和泉志』 }} とあり長谷川[2007: 49]。、これに従った記述が[[寛政]]7年（[[1795年]]）の『和泉名所図会』にも見られる。 [[明治]]以降も厩戸王子神社ないし筆王子神社の名で存続し、石禿倉（ほくら）と呼ばれる小石祠が祀られていたが、[[1907年]]（[[明治40年]]）12月9日に[[一岡神社]]に合祀され（『史跡名勝天然記念物』）、市杵島神社という境内社になっている。 * 所在地 泉南市信達大苗代 File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|厩戸王子跡の石碑（大阪府泉南市信達大苗代）
{{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|[[一岡神社]]の境内社、市杵島神社。右側奥は[[海会寺跡]] === 信達王子 === 厩戸王子近くの海営宮池から旧街道を約2km西南に進み、牧野を過ぎた辺りに跡地がある。東西2[[間]]半・南北2間半、広さ約6坪 西[1987: 89]。の跡地に地蔵石像、馬頭観音像等が祀られている。史料によって名称に混乱があり、信達王子だけでなく、信達一之瀬王子、一之瀬王子などの名称が知られている長谷川[2007: 51]、西[1987: 89]は「信達王子」、平凡社[1998: 1526]、「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会[1983: 1554]は「一之瀬王子」を、それぞれ採用し、他を別称として付記している。。 後鳥羽院参詣記は「信達一之瀬王子」、修明門院参詣記は「信達王子」としている平凡社[1998: 1526]。。さらに[[文明 (日本)|文明]]6年（[[1474年]]）の『王子記』には信達王子と長岡王子の2社が併記され、[[享保]]15年（[[1730年]]）の「九十九王子記」に {{quotation|信達王子、在牧野村。一瀬王子、在所信達庄市場村。長岡王子、在岡村北坂中。|「九十九王子記」（享保15年）}} との記述が見られる長谷川[2007: 51]。。『大阪府史跡名称天然記念物』は、「長岡王子」の呼称が、信達王子近辺の旧街道の坂道が長岡と呼ばれたことから生じた別称であるとしている。 明治初期まで小祠が祀られていたというが、[[1910年]]（[[明治43年]]）に[[信達神社]]（泉南市信達金熊寺）に合祀された。 * 所在地 泉南市信達牧野 File:信達王子跡.jpeg|信達王子跡と熊野街道碑 === 長岡王子 === 泉南市内にあったと推定される王子社。いくつかの史料や研究において実在のものとされるが詳細は明らかではない。[[#信達王子|信達王子]]の節に述べたように、近世の幾つかの史料に名が見られるほか、『紀伊名所図会』も長岡王子を信達王子とは別個の王子社として掲げている西 [1987: 32]。。 File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|長岡王子跡顕彰碑と鳥居 == 注 == {{Reflist|2}} == 参考文献 == * 長谷川 靖高、2007、『熊野王子巡拝ガイドブック』、新風書房 ISBN 9784882696292 * 平凡社、1998、『大阪府の地名』、平凡社（日本歴史地名大系28） ISBN 458249028X * 「角川日本地名大辞典」編纂委員会、1983、『大阪府』、角川書店（角川日本地名大辞典27） * 西 律、1987、『熊野古道みちしるべ - 熊野九十九王子現状踏査録』、荒尾成文堂（みなもと選書1） == 関連項目 == * [[熊野信仰]] * [[熊野古道]] - [[紀伊路]] * [[九十九王子]] ** （[[九十九王子 (泉佐野市)#籾井王子|籾井王子]]） - '''厩戸王子''' - '''信達一ノ瀬王子''' - '''長岡王子''' - （[[九十九王子 (阪南市)#地蔵堂王子|地蔵堂王子]]） * [[山中宿]]・[[信達宿]] - 旧紀州街道の宿場跡 {{九十九王子}} {{DEFAULTSORT:くしゆうくおうし}} [[Category:九十九王子|せんなんし]] [[Category:大阪府の神社|くしゆうくおうしせんなんし]] [[Category:泉南市の歴史]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[九十九王子 (泉南市)]] to [[List of Kujūku Ōji (Sennan City)]] without leaving a redirect: Bot: merge import

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
