revision.comment: Text replacement - "Abeno Ōji Shrine" to "Abe Ōji Shrine"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|qq=draft}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|qq=draft}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.comment: Bot: add simplewiki draft link to ill templates
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}} - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka-juku|qq=}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi-juku|qq=}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.comment: Text replacement - "|明治時代|" to "|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kumano Three Shrines]] → [[Kumano shrine]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.comment: Bot: fix redirect [[Kujūku Ōji template]] → [[Template:Kujūku Ōji]]
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of {{ill|Sennan City|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan City|qq=}}
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within {{ill|Sennan, Osaka|ja|泉南市|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sennan, Osaka|qq=}}, Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Kujūku Ōji? == {{main|Kujūku Ōji}}
The {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of {{ill|Shinto shrines|ja|神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shinto shrines|qq=}} located along the {{ill|Kumano Kodo|ja|熊野古道|1=Kumano Kodō|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodo|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Kodō|qq=}}, particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the {{ill|Jōkyū War|ja|承久の乱|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Jōkyū War|qq=}} in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Muromachi period|qq=}}, the decline accelerated. Although the {{ill|Kishu Domain|ja|紀州藩|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Domain|qq=}} attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of {{ill|State Shinto|ja|国家神道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/State Shinto|qq=}} in the {{ill|Meiji era|ja|明治|lt=Meiji period|1=Meiji era|2=ja|3=明治|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meiji era|qq=}} led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Kujūku Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Kujūku Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor {{ill|Go-Toba|ja|後鳥羽天皇|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Go-Toba|qq=}} in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of {{ill|Kaieiji Temple|ja|海会寺跡|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kaieiji Temple|qq=}} are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a {{ill|Batō Kannon|ja|馬頭観音|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Batō Kannon|qq=}} statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}

Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Kujūku Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).

revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka-juku|qq=}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi-juku|qq=}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.comment: Text replacement - "九十九王子|" to "九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|qq=}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka-juku|qq=}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi-juku|qq=}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

revision.comment: Text replacement - "Kujūku Ōji|" to "Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|"
revision.text:
This is a list of some of the {{ill|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Kujūku Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Kujūku Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
