<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|qid=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|qq=|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|qq=|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|qq=|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|qq=|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka-juku|1=Yamanaka-juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466142}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi-juku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
== References == {{Reflist|2}}
== Bibliography == * Hasegawa, Yasutaka (2007). *Guidebook to Kumano Ōji Pilgrimage*. Shinpū Shobō. ISBN 9784882696292 * Heibonsha (1998). *Osaka Prefecture Place Names*. Heibonsha (Nihon Rekishi Chimei Taikei 28). ISBN 458249028X * Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee (1983). *Osaka Prefecture*. Kadokawa Shoten (Kadokawa Nihon Chimei Daijiten 27). * Nishi, Ritsu (1987). *Guide to Kumano Kodo - Field Survey of Ninety-Nine Ōji*. Arao Seibundō (Minamoto Selection 1).
== See Also == * {{ill|Kumano Faith|ja|熊野信仰|1=Kumano Faith|2=ja|3=熊野信仰|WD=Q135501741}}{{efn|The religion of [[Kumano Shrines]]|1=Kumano shrine|lt=Kumano Three Shrines|lt=Kumano faith|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Faith|13=User:Immanuelle/Kumano Three Shrines|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 熊野三山}} * [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]] - {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}} * {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} * {{ill|Yamanaka-juku|ja|山中宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Yamanaka-juku|1=Yamanaka-juku|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11466142}}, {{ill|Shindachi-juku|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi-juku|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} - former post towns on the Kishu Kaidō

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[ja:九十九王子 (泉南市)]] {{Infobox religious building|prefecture={{ill|Sennan|WD=Q838809}}|coordinates={{Coord|34.3772|N|135.2867|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}}}}{{afc comment|Wikidata entry [[da:d:Q11370049]] Wikidata name: (none) [[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:52, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} This is a list of some of the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|fr|Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=99 ōji Shrines|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} on the [[Kumano Kodō]]. This article covers the ōji Shrines in the region of [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan City]]
==Overview== {{Redirect|Umayado Prince|Umayado '''Emperor''' Prince|Shōtoku Taishi}} This article discusses the {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} (Ninety-Nine Ōji) located within [[:en:Sennan, Osaka|Sennan, Osaka]], Osaka Prefecture.
== What is Ninety-Nine Ōji? == {{main|Ninety-Nine Ōji}}
The {{ill|Ninety-Nine Ōji|ja|九十九王子|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|2=ja|3=九十九王子|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|4=fr|5=Kujūkuōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji|1=Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|13=User:Immanuelle/Ninety-Nine Ōji|lt=Ninety-Nine Ōji|qq=}} refers to a group of [[:en:Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] located along the [[:en:Kumano Kodō|Kumano Kodō]], particularly the {{ill|Kii Route|ja|紀伊路|1=Kiiji|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Route|13=User:Immanuelle/Kiiji|qq=}}, which were rapidly organized primarily by Kumano Shugendō guides in the 12th and 13th centuries to assist aristocrats and imperial family members on their pilgrimages to Kumano. These shrines were places of worship and protection for pilgrims.
However, after the [[:en:Jōkyū War|Jōkyū War]] in 1221, the number of pilgrims from {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} to Kumano declined, leading to the deterioration of the Kii Route and the gradual abandonment of these shrines. As Kumano pilgrimages lost their former prestige in the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]], the decline accelerated. Although the [[:en:Kishū Domain|Kishu Domain]] attempted to preserve them in the Edo period, their influence never fully recovered. Furthermore, the rise of [[:en:State Shinto|State Shinto]] in the [[:en:Meiji era|Meiji period]] led to shrine mergers and urbanization, causing many former shrine sites to disappear or their locations to become unclear.
This article covers the Ninety-Nine Ōji whose locations are estimated to be within Sennan, Osaka.
== Ninety-Nine Ōji in Sennan City == There are three Ōji shrines in Sennan City.
=== Umayado Ōji === Traveling west about 1.5 km along the old {{ill|Kishu Kaidō|ja|紀州街道|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kishu Kaidō|1=Kishū Kaidō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11605050}} from {{ill|Momii Ōji|ja|籾井王子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Momii Ōji|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 九十九王子 (泉佐野市)|1=List of Ninety-Nine Ōji Shrines (Izumisano City)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11370048}}, then turning north at an intersection near the Kaiemiyama Pond, one reaches a cemetery. North of this, there is a stone monument and an explanatory sign. This site is designated as a historical site by Osaka Prefecture.
In the pilgrimage record of Emperor [[:en:Emperor Go-Toba|Go-Toba]] in 1201 (Ken'nin 1), found in the {{ill|Meigetsuki|ja|明月記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Meigetsuki|qq=}}, an entry on October 7 states: *"First visit to Umayado Ōji"* After performing an entertainment dedication at Momii Ōji, the emperor visited this shrine and stayed overnight at the {{ill|Shindachi Inn|ja|信達宿|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Inn|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 信達村|1=Shindachi|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11386271}} Umayado residence.<ref name="taikei_1527">Heibonsha [1998: 1527]</ref>.
Similarly, in 1210 (Jōgen 4), the pilgrimage record of {{ill|Fujiwara no Shigeko|ja|藤原重子|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Fujiwara no Shigeko|1=Fujiwara no Shigeko|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11623607}}, recorded in the {{ill|Minkeiki|ja|民経記|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Minkeiki|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to 広橋経光|1=Hirohashi Tsunemitsu|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q20042592}}, notes that she visited this shrine before retiring for the night.<ref name="taikei_1527"/>.
After these mentions, historical records about this Ōji shrine cease. However, in 1736 (Genbun 1), the "Izumi-shi" describes the site as:
{{quotation|…Located in Nakakōji Village. The Ōji records call it Umayado, or sometimes Umadome. Nowadays, it is known as Fude Ōji.|"Izumi-shi"}}
This information is also reflected in the 1795 (Kansei 7) "Izumi Meisho Zue" (Famous Sites of Izumi).<ref name="Hasegawa_49">Hasegawa [2007: 49]</ref>.
During the Meiji era, the shrine continued under the name Umayado Ōji Shrine or Fude Ōji Shrine, with a small stone shrine called "Hokura" being worshipped. However, on December 9, 1907 (Meiji 40), it was merged into {{ill|Ichioka Shrine|ja|一岡神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichioka Shrine|1=Ichioka Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11352633}} and became a subsidiary shrine dedicated to {{ill|Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrines|lt=Ichikishima Shrine|ja|市杵島神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ichikishima Shrine|qq=}}.<ref name="Hasegawa_49"/>.
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:Ruins of Umayado-oji Shrine.JPG|Stone monument at the site of Umayado Ōji (Shindachi Onawashiro, Sennan, Osaka) {{Coord|34.3772|135.2867|format=dms|dscale:30000}} File:Ichikijima Shrine in Ichioka Shrine.JPG|Ichikishima Shrine, a subsidiary shrine of Ichioka Shrine. The remains of [[:en:Kaie-ji temple ruins|Kaieiji Temple]] are visible in the background. </gallery>
=== Shindachi Ōji === Located about 2 km southwest of Umayado Ōji along the old road, near Makino. The shrine site measures 2.5 ken (about 4.5 meters) east-west and 2.5 ken north-south, covering approximately 6 tsubo (20 square meters). It contains a stone statue of Jizō, a [[:en:Hayagriva (Buddhism)|Batō Kannon]] statue, and other figures.<ref> Nishi [1987: 89]</ref>.
There is some confusion over its name, as different sources refer to it as Shindachi Ōji, Shindachi Ichinose Ōji, or Ichinose Ōji.<ref>Hasegawa [2007: 51]; Nishi [1987: 89] uses "Shindachi Ōji," while Heibonsha [1998: 1526] and the Kadokawa Japan Place Name Dictionary Editorial Committee [1983: 1554] use "Ichinose Ōji."</ref>.
Emperor Go-Toba's pilgrimage record refers to it as "Shindachi Ichinose Ōji," while Fujiwara no Shigeko's record calls it "Shindachi Ōji."<ref name="heibonsha_1526">Heibonsha [1998: 1526]</ref>.
By the early Meiji era, a small shrine still existed at the site<ref name="heibonsha_1526"/> but was merged into {{ill|Shindachi Shrine|ja|信達神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shindachi Shrine|1=Shindachi Shrine|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q65248875}} in 1910 (Meiji 43).
* '''Location:''' Shindachi Makino, Sennan, Osaka <gallery> File:信達王子跡.jpeg|Monument marking the site of Shindachi Ōji and a Kumano Kodo milestone </gallery>
=== Nagaoka Ōji === Believed to have existed in Sennan, Osaka, though its precise location remains unclear. Several historical documents and studies mention it as a real shrine, separate from Shindachi Ōji. The {{ill|Kii Meisho Zue|ja|紀伊名所図会|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kii Meisho Zue|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} also lists Nagaoka Ōji separately.<ref>Nishi [1987: 32]</ref>. <gallery> File:長岡王子跡 02.jpg|Nagaoka Ōji memorial stone and torii gate </gallery>
